analogy: Think of "Balance of Payments" through the chapter's own opening images: Chapter 5Chapter 5 Governmen Governmen Governmen Governmen Government tt tt Budget Budget Budget Budget Budget and the Economy and the Economy and the Economy and the Economy and the Economy We introduced the government in chapter one as denoting the state. We stated that apart... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 5.1 GOVERNMENT BUDGET — MEANING AND ITS COMPONENTS, 5.1.1 Objectives of Government Budget, 5.1.2 Classification of Receipts, 5.2 BALANCED, SURPLUS AND DEFICIT BUDGET. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Balance of Payments is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that chapter 5Chapter 5 Governmen Governmen Governmen Governmen Government tt tt Budget Budget Budget Budget Budget and the Economy and the Economy and the Economy and the Economy and the Economy We introduced the government in chapter one as denoting the state.

The text explains that we stated that apart from the private sector, there is the government which plays a very important role.

The text explains that an economy in which there is both the private sector and the Government is known as a mixed economy.

The text explains that there are many ways in which the government influences economic life.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will limit ourselves to the functions which are carried on through the government budget.

The text explains that in section 5.1 we present the components of the government budget to bring out the sources of government revenue and avenues of government spending.

The text explains that in section 5.2 we discuss the topic of balanced, surplus or deficit budget to account for the difference between expenditures and revenue collection.

The text explains that it specifically deals with the meaning of different kinds of budget deficits, their implications and the measures to contain them.

The text explains that 5.1 deals with fiscal policy and a simple description of the multiplier.

The text explains that the role the government plays has implications for its deficits which further affect its debt- what the government owes.

The text explains that the chapter concludes with an analysis of the debt issue.

The text explains that 5.1 GOVERNMENT BUDGET — MEANING AND ITS COMPONENTS There is a constitutional requirement in India (Article 112) to present before the Parliament a statement of estimated receipts and expenditures of the government in respect of every financial year which runs from 1 April to 31 March.

The text explains that this ‘Annual Financial Statement’ constitutes the main budget document of the government.

The text explains that although the budget document relates to the receipts and expenditure of the government for a particular financial year, the impact of it will be there in subsequent years.

The text explains that there is a need therefore to have two accounts- those that relate to the current financial year only are included in the revenue account (also called revenue budget) and those that concern the assets and liabilities of the government into the capital account (also called capital budget).

The text explains that in order to understand the accounts, it is important to first understand the objectives of the government budget.

The text explains that 5.1.1 Objectives of Government Budget The government plays a very important role in increasing the welfare of the people.

The text explains that in order to do that the government intervenes in the economy in the following ways.

The text explains that allocation Function of Government Budget Government provides certain goods and services which cannot be provided by the market mechanism i.e. by exchange between individual consumers and producers.

The text explains that examples of such goods are national defence, roads, government administration etc. which are referred to as public goods.

The text explains that to understand why public goods need to be provided by the government, we must understand the difference between private goods such as clothes, cars, food items etc. and public goods.

The text explains that the link between the producer and consumer which occurs through the payment process is broken and the government must step in to provide for such goods.

The text explains that public provision means that they are financed through the budget and can be used without any direct payment.

The text explains that public goods may be produced by the government or the private sector.

The text explains that when goods are produced directly by the government it is called public production.

The text explains that redistribution Function of Government Budget From chapter two we know that the total national income of the country goes to either the private sector, that is, firms and households (known as private income) or the government (known as public income).

The text explains that the government sector affects the personal disposable income of households by making transfers and collecting taxes.

The text explains that it is through this that the government can change the distribution of income and bring about a distribution that is considered ‘fair’ by society.

The text explains that 6 7 6 7 6 7 6 7 6 7 Government Budget and the Economy

The text explains that 68 68 68 68 68 Introductory Macroeconomics Stabilisation Function of Government Budget The government may need to correct fluctuations in income and employment.

The text explains that the overall level of employment and prices in the economy depends upon the level of aggregate demand which depends on the spending decisions of millions of private economic agents apart from the government.

The text explains that in any period, the level of demand may not be sufficient for full utilisation of labour and other resources of the economy.

The text explains that the government needs to intervene to raise the aggregate demand.

The text explains that the intervention of the government whether to expand demand or reduce it constitutes the stabilisation function.

The text explains that 5.1.2 Classification of Receipts Revenue Receipts: Revenue receipts are those receipts that do not lead to a claim on the government.

The text explains that tax revenues, an important component of revenue receipts, have for long been divided into direct taxes (personal income tax) and firms (corporation tax), and indirect taxes like excise taxes (duties levied on goods produced within the country), customs duties (taxes imposed on goods imported into and exported out of India) and service tax1.

The text explains that other direct taxes like wealth tax, gift tax and estate duty (now abolished) have never brought in large amount of revenue and thus have been referred to as ‘paper taxes’.

The text explains that non-tax revenue of the central government mainly consists of interest receipts on account of loans by the central government, dividends and profits on investments made by the government, fees and other receipts for services rendered by the government.

The text explains that capital Receipts: The government also receives money by way of loans or from the sale of its assets.

The text explains that (b) If government expenditure increases by 30, what is the impact on equilibrium income?

The text explains that (c) If a lump-sum tax of 30 is added to pay for the increase in government purchases, how will equilibrium income change?

The text explains that in the above question, calculate the effect on output of a 10 per cent increase in transfers, and a 10 per cent increase in lump-sum taxes.

The text explains that we suppose that C = 70 + 0.70Y D, I = 90, G = 100, T = 0.10Y (a) Find the equilibrium income.

The text explains that (b) What are tax revenues at equilibrium income?

The text explains that suppose marginal propensity to consume is 0.75 and there is a 20 per cent proportional income tax.

The text explains that find the change in equilibrium income for the following (a) Government purchases increase by 20 (b) Transfers decrease by 20.

The text explains that explain why the tax multiplier is smaller in absolute value than the government expenditure multiplier.

The text explains that explain the relation between government deficit and government debt.

The text explains that how good is the system of G.S.T as compared to the old tax system?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Introductory Macroeconomics, Chapter 5: Balance of Payments

prelims_traps: Do not treat 5.1 GOVERNMENT BUDGET — MEANING AND ITS COMPONENTS and 5.1.1 Objectives of Government Budget as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Balance of Payments; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Balance of Payments through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 5.1 GOVERNMENT BUDGET — MEANING AND ITS COMPONENTS and 5.1.1 Objectives of Government Budget to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
